skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides developers through a four-phase debugging process that requires root cause investigation before fixes.
skill.summary: Use root cause analysis, pattern review, hypothesis testing, and verification to debug software issues systematically.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly found command-like text, filesystem path text, and macOS keychain diagnostic commands, but the skill is Markdown guidance rather than executable code. Most findings are false positives from examples and test scenarios; the remaining concern is that users may follow diagnostic shell examples that reveal environment or signing identity metadata.

medium_findings.title: Diagnostic Commands May Reveal Local System Metadata
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with benign intent. The skill includes a bash diagnostic example using environment checks, macOS keychain listing, signing identity discovery, and codesign. These commands are not executed by the skill, but if followed without review they can expose local configuration and signing identity metadata in logs or chat transcripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in a Markdown code block and serve a debugging purpose. The risk is real but limited because there is no automatic execution or network exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Pressure Test Prompts Use Directive Scenario Language
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW with low operational impact. The pressure-test files tell the reader to choose and act in staged debugging scenarios. This is not an instruction to skip security review, but marketplace consumers should understand these files are evaluation prompts, not runtime policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imperative wording is clearly part of test scenarios. I found no evidence that it attempts to override marketplace audit instructions or claim special authority.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Path Mention Is Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The hidden filesystem path is a source attribution in the creation log, not code that reads or writes a hidden file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain Markdown prose. It contains no file access operation, command invocation, or data transfer.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is Textual False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static weak-crypto hits occur on prose lines such as the skill description and creation notes. I found no cryptographic API call, hashing operation, or instruction to use weak algorithms at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines showed normal debugging prose or a signing command example. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Findings Are Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for direct execution. The detected backticks are Markdown fences, inline code spans, or scenario examples. They do not execute Ruby or shell code inside the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documents and the cited content is fenced or inline example text. One example still has medium risk because users may copy diagnostic commands.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Scenario or Diagnostic Context
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious reconnaissance. The system and network reconnaissance hits are either debugging diagnostics or pressure-test scenario text. I found no evidence of scanning targets, network probing, or automated data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines refer to debugging decisions, not reconnaissance commands. No scanner, connection tool, or external endpoint appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: At Scheduling Finding Is Natural Language
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The at-command scheduling finding appears on a sentence about dinner plans and code review timing, not a command that schedules jobs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is ordinary scenario text. It contains no shell syntax, no at invocation, and no scheduled task payload.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Shell Diagnostics in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains copyable shell diagnostics involving environment variables, macOS keychain listing, signing identity discovery, and codesign. This is legitimate debugging guidance, but users should review and redact diagnostic output before sharing it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present and has a plausible information exposure risk. There is no evidence of automatic execution or outbound transmission.

content.value_statement: Random fixes often hide the real cause and create new defects. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured process for finding root causes before changing code.
actual_capabilities: Guides root cause investigation before proposing fixes.
Prompts review of error messages, recent changes, and reproduction steps.
Compares failing behavior against working patterns in the codebase.
Structures one-hypothesis-at-a-time debugging and minimal tests.
Requires a failing test or reproduction before implementation.
Identifies when repeated failed fixes may indicate architecture problems.
limitations: It is a process guide, not an automated debugger.
It may slow down very small fixes that already have clear evidence.
It references other skills that may not be installed.
Diagnostic examples may expose local metadata if copied without review.

use_cases.title: Fix a failing feature without guessing
use_cases.description: Use the four phases to reproduce the bug, trace the failing path, and make one evidence-based fix.

use_cases.title: Investigate flaky or failing tests
use_cases.description: Separate symptoms from root causes by comparing environments, timing, dependencies, and recent changes.

use_cases.title: Debug incidents under pressure
use_cases.description: Keep incident work focused on evidence, component boundaries, and verified recovery instead of quick patches.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Bug Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging for this bug report: the login button does nothing. Start with root cause investigation and do not propose a fix yet.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Test Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: A unit or integration test fails.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging for this failing test: expected completed status but received pending status. Identify evidence needed before any code change.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a Multi-Component Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: A failure crosses service or build boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging to investigate a CI build that passes locally but fails during signing. Map each component boundary and evidence gap.

prompt_templates.title: Recover After Failed Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Several attempted fixes did not work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging after three failed attempts. Reassess the evidence, question architecture assumptions, and define the next verified hypothesis.

output_examples.input: The build fails with a missing module error.
output: Phase 1: Confirm the exact error, import path, dependency state, and recent changes.
Phase 2: Compare the failing import with working imports in the same project.
Phase 3: State one cause to test, such as a missing dependency or wrong alias.
Phase 4: Add a focused reproduction, apply one fix, and verify the build.

output_examples.input: Tests pass locally but time out in CI.
output: Check CI logs, environment differences, service startup timing, and recent pipeline changes.
Compare against a working CI job before changing timeout values.
Test one hypothesis, such as a missing service readiness check.
Replace arbitrary waits with a verified condition-based fix.

output_examples.input: A production API is returning intermittent payment failures.
output: Read the full error and identify which component reports the failure.
Gather request, service, and provider evidence without exposing secrets.
Form a single hypothesis about the failing boundary.
Implement the smallest verified recovery and monitor for recurrence.

best_practices: Capture evidence before proposing any fix.
Change one variable per hypothesis test.
Redact diagnostic output before sharing logs or command results.
anti_patterns: Adding a quick workaround before understanding why the failure occurs.
Bundling several fixes into one change and losing test clarity.
Copying diagnostic commands into sensitive environments without review.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides a four-phase debugging process: investigation, pattern analysis, hypothesis testing, and implementation.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance. Users still decide whether to run any diagnostic command.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes shell diagnostics that can reveal environment and signing metadata if copied without review.

faq.question: Can I use it during incidents?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to keep incident debugging evidence-based when time pressure encourages guessing.

faq.question: Does it replace TDD?
faq.answer: No. It uses failing tests as part of debugging, but it focuses on finding root causes.

faq.question: Which tools support it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
